content.text:
### Setup Step for Script Mode (YAML)

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/scratchpad/actions.md

Defines the setup step executed in script mode. It runs a local setup script (`setup.sh`) from the checked-out actions source, copying necessary scripts to the destination.

```yaml
- name: Setup Scripts
  run: |
    bash /tmp/gh-aw/actions-source/actions/setup/setup.sh
  env:
    INPUT_DESTINATION: /opt/gh-aw/actions
```

--------------------------------

### Bash: Setup Scripts for GitHub Actions

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/scratchpad/actions.md

This bash script is used in GitHub Actions to set up scripts for subsequent steps. It takes a destination directory as an input environment variable.

```bash
bash /tmp/gh-aw/actions-source/actions/setup/setup.sh
        env:
          INPUT_DESTINATION: /opt/gh-aw/actions
```

--------------------------------

### Install gh-aw CLI Action with Complete Workflow

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/actions/setup-cli/README.md

This YAML snippet shows a complete GitHub Actions workflow that includes checking out the repository, installing the gh-aw CLI using the 'setup-cli' action, and verifying the installation by running version and help commands.

```yaml
name: Test gh-aw

on: [push]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Checkout repository
        uses: actions/checkout@v6
      
      - name: Install gh-aw
        uses: github/gh-aw/actions/setup-cli@main
        with:
          version: v0.37.18
      
      - name: Verify installation
        run: |
          gh aw version
          gh aw --help
```

--------------------------------

### Build gh-aw CLI Action Installation Script

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/actions/setup-cli/README.md

This bash command is used during the development process to build the GitHub Action. It copies the main installation script from the repository root to the action's directory.

```bash
make build  # Copies install-gh-aw.sh to actions/setup-cli/install.sh
```

### GitHub Actions Directory > Available Actions > setup

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/actions/README.md

The `setup` action copies workflow script files to the agent environment. It embeds all necessary JavaScript files used across all workflow jobs and copies them to a specified destination directory.

content.text:
### Implement My AI Engine Installation Steps in Go

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/scratchpad/adding-new-engines.md

Overrides the `GetInstallationSteps` function to generate the necessary steps for installing the My AI Engine. It handles secret validation, package installation (e.g., npm), and optional firewall agent installation based on workflow configurations. Dependencies include utility functions for generating secret validation and package installation steps.

```go
func (e *MyEngine) GetInstallationSteps(workflowData *WorkflowData) []GitHubActionStep {
    myEngineLog.Printf("Generating installation steps: workflow=%s", workflowData.Name)

    // Skip installation if custom command is specified
    if workflowData.EngineConfig != nil && workflowData.EngineConfig.Command != "" {
        myEngineLog.Printf("Skipping installation: custom command specified (%s)", workflowData.EngineConfig.Command)
        return []GitHubActionStep{}
    }

    var steps []GitHubActionStep

    // Add secret validation step
    secretValidation := GenerateMultiSecretValidationStep(
        []string{"MY_ENGINE_API_KEY"},
        "My AI Engine",
        "https://github.github.com/gh-aw/reference/engines/#my-ai-engine",
    )
    steps = append(steps, secretValidation)

    // Determine engine version
    version := "1.0.0" // Default version
    if workflowData.EngineConfig != nil && workflowData.EngineConfig.Version != "" {
        version = workflowData.EngineConfig.Version
    }

    // Add installation steps (example: npm package)
    npmSteps := GenerateNpmInstallSteps(
        "@my-company/my-engine",
        version,
        "Install My AI Engine",
        "my-engine",
        true, // Include Node.js setup
    )
    steps = append(steps, npmSteps...)

    // Add firewall installation if enabled
    if isFirewallEnabled(workflowData) {
        firewallConfig := getFirewallConfig(workflowData)
        agentConfig := getAgentConfig(workflowData)
        var awfVersion string
        if firewallConfig != nil {
            awfVersion = firewallConfig.Version
        }

        awfInstall := generateAWFInstallationStep(awfVersion, agentConfig)
        if len(awfInstall) > 0 {
            steps = append(steps, awfInstall)
        }
    }

    return steps
}
```

--------------------------------

### Generate npm Package Installation Steps (JavaScript)

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/scratchpad/adding-new-engines.md

Provides a JavaScript function 'GenerateNpmInstallSteps' to create GitHub Action steps for installing an npm package. It includes options for specifying the package name, version, display name, CLI command, and whether to include Node.js setup.

```javascript
npmSteps := GenerateNpmInstallSteps(
    "@company/engine",
    version,
    "Install Engine",
    "engine-cli",
    true, // Include Node.js setup
)
steps = append(steps, npmSteps...)
```

--------------------------------

### Install Dependencies (npm)

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/docs/README.md

Installs all the necessary project dependencies listed in the package.json file. This command is essential before running any other project commands.

```bash
npm install
```

--------------------------------

### Install gh-aw CLI Extension

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/scratchpad/oh-my-code.md

Instructions for installing the GitHub Agentic Workflows CLI extension using either the `gh` command or npm. This extension is necessary for managing and interacting with agentic workflows.

```bash
# Install gh-aw CLI extension
gh extension install github/gh-aw

# Or use npm
npm install -g gh-aw
```

### gh-aw > Common Patterns > npm Package Installation

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/scratchpad/adding-new-engines.md

For engines that rely on npm packages, the `GenerateNpmInstallSteps()` helper function simplifies the installation process. This function takes the package name, version, a display name for the step, the CLI command, and a boolean indicating whether to include Node.js setup. It returns a slice of `GitHubActionStep` that can be appended to the workflow's steps.

engine: claude

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/docs/src/content/docs/reference/auth.mdx

Setup: Create an API key at https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/get-started. Add it to your repository secrets, either by CLI or GitHub UI: gh aw secrets set ANTHROPIC_API_KEY --value "YOUR_ANTHROPIC_API_KEY". See also Using Claude Code for additional configuration needed when using Claude with GitHub MCP.

But wait - the issue is NOT in gh-aw's entrypoint itself. The entrypoint is just using the PATH that was set up by gh-aw. Let me re-examine:

[entrypoint] Executing command: /bin/bash -c /bin/bash -c 'export PATH="$(find /opt/hostedtoolcache -maxdepth 4 -type d -name bin 2>/dev/null | tr '\''\n'\'' '\'':'\'')$PATH"; ... claude --print --no-chrome ...
```

This entrypoint is the **AWF** (Agent Workflow Firewall) entrypoint, not gh-sr's entrypoint. The AWF container's entrypoint is what sets up the PATH.

So the question is: why doesn't the AWF container have claude in PATH when running on a self-hosted runner vs GitHub hosted runners?

content.text:
### Configure Custom AWF Agent with Command, Args, and Env

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/pkg/cli/workflows/test-custom-agent.md

This YAML configuration defines a custom agent for AWF. It specifies a Docker command to run a custom AWF image, along with additional arguments and environment variables for enhanced logging and debugging.

```yaml
sandbox:
  agent:
    id: awf
    command: "docker run --rm -it my-custom-awf-image"
    args:
      - "--custom-logging"
      - "--debug-mode"
    env:
      AWF_CUSTOM_VAR: "custom_value"
      DEBUG_LEVEL: "verbose"
```

--------------------------------

### Agentic Workflow Firewall (AWF) Configuration

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/docs/src/content/docs/reference/glossary.md

The Agent Workflow Firewall (AWF) provides a sandboxed environment for AI agent execution with controlled network egress. This example shows how to enable AWF, which is the default when `sandbox` is not specified.

```yaml
sandbox:
  agent: awf
  # Optional: specify allowed domains for network egress
  allowed_domains:
    - api.example.com
    - github.com
```

--------------------------------

### AWF Custom Agent Example with Command, Args, and Env

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/pkg/cli/workflows/test-custom-agent.md

An example YAML snippet showcasing the custom agent configuration for AWF. It includes a custom command, arguments for debug mode and verbosity, and an environment variable for log level.

```yaml
sandbox:
  agent:
    id: awf
    command: "docker run --rm my-custom-awf"
    args: ["--debug-mode", "--verbose"]
    env:
      AWF_LOG_LEVEL: "debug"
```

--------------------------------

### Custom AWF Wrapper Configuration (YAML)

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/docs/src/content/docs/reference/sandbox.md

This snippet illustrates a custom AWF setup. It specifies a custom wrapper command, includes custom arguments for logging and debugging, and sets custom environment variables for the agent.

```yaml
sandbox:
  agent:
    id: awf
    command: "/usr/local/bin/custom-awf-wrapper"
    args:
      - "--custom-logging"
      - "--debug-mode"
    env:
      AWF_CUSTOM_VAR: "custom_value"
      DEBUG_LEVEL: "verbose"
```

### Advanced Features > AWF (Agent Workflow Firewall)

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/docs/src/content/docs/reference/glossary.md

The Agent Workflow Firewall (AWF) is the default coding agent sandbox. It isolates AI agent execution within a container, controlling network egress through domain-based access lists. AWF makes the host filesystem and environment variables accessible inside the container while restricting outbound network access solely to configured domains. It is enabled by setting `sandbox.agent: awf` (which is the default when `sandbox` is not explicitly specified). Consult [Sandbox Configuration](/gh-aw/reference/sandbox/) for further details.

content.text:
### Configure Custom AWF Agent with Command, Args, and Env

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/pkg/cli/workflows/test-custom-agent.md

This YAML configuration defines a custom agent for AWF. It specifies a Docker command to run a custom AWF image, along with additional arguments and environment variables for enhanced logging and debugging.

```yaml
sandbox:
  agent:
    id: awf
    command: "docker run --rm -it my-custom-awf-image"
    args:
      - "--custom-logging"
      - "--debug-mode"
    env:
      AWF_CUSTOM_VAR: "custom_value"
      DEBUG_LEVEL: "verbose"
```

--------------------------------

### Configure Docker Container MCP Servers

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/docs/src/content/docs/guides/mcps.md

This configuration shows how to run MCP servers within Docker containers. It allows specifying the container image, environment variables, volume mounts, entrypoint arguments, and network restrictions for secure and isolated execution.

```yaml
mcp-servers:
  ast-grep:
    container: "mcp/ast-grep:latest"
    allowed: ["*"]

  custom-tool:
    container: "mcp/custom-tool:v1.0"
    args: ["-v", "/host/data:/app/data"]  # Volume mounts before image
    entrypointArgs: ["serve", "--port", "8080"]  # App args after image
    env:
      API_KEY: "${{ secrets.API_KEY }}"
    allowed: ["tool1", "tool2"]

network:
  allowed:
    - defaults
    - api.example.com  # Restricts egress to allowed domains
```

--------------------------------

### AWF Custom Agent Example with Command, Args, and Env

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/pkg/cli/workflows/test-custom-agent.md

An example YAML snippet showcasing the custom agent configuration for AWF. It includes a custom command, arguments for debug mode and verbosity, and an environment variable for log level.

```yaml
sandbox:
  agent:
    id: awf
    command: "docker run --rm my-custom-awf"
    args: ["--debug-mode", "--verbose"]
    env:
      AWF_LOG_LEVEL: "debug"
```

--------------------------------

### Custom AWF Wrapper Configuration (YAML)

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/docs/src/content/docs/reference/sandbox.md

This snippet illustrates a custom AWF setup. It specifies a custom wrapper command, includes custom arguments for logging and debugging, and sets custom environment variables for the agent.

```yaml
sandbox:
  agent:
    id: awf
    command: "/usr/local/bin/custom-awf-wrapper"
    args:
      - "--custom-logging"
      - "--debug-mode"
    env:
      AWF_CUSTOM_VAR: "custom_value"
      DEBUG_LEVEL: "verbose"
```

### Sandbox Configuration > Coding Agent Sandbox Types > AWF (Agent Workflow Firewall)

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/docs/src/content/docs/reference/sandbox.md

AWF is the default coding agent sandbox that provides network egress control through domain-based access controls. Network permissions are configured through the top-level `network` field. AWF makes the host filesystem visible inside the container with appropriate permissions for user paths (read-write), system paths (read-only), and hides the Docker socket for security. All host binaries are available without explicit mounts, and environment variables are passed via `--env-all`, with the host `PATH` captured as `AWF_HOST_PATH` and restored inside the container.

content.text:
### GitHub Actions Workflow for PR Creation

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/pkg/cli/workflows/test-claude-create-pull-request.md

This YAML defines a GitHub Actions workflow triggered manually. It sets permissions for pull requests and contents, specifies the 'claude' engine, and outlines the steps to create a test pull request.

```yaml
on:
  workflow_dispatch:
permissions:
  pull-requests: read
  contents: read
engine: claude
---

# Test Claude Create Pull Request

This is a test workflow to verify that Claude can create new pull requests.

Please create a new pull request that:
1. Creates a new branch called "test-branch"
2. Adds a simple README.md change
3. Creates a PR with the title "Test PR from Claude"
4. Includes a proper description explaining this is a test PR
```

--------------------------------

### Update Release Safe Output Configuration

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/pkg/cli/workflows/test-claude-update-release.md

Configures the 'update-release' safe output, specifying the engine to be used (Claude) and setting a maximum limit for updates.

```yaml
engine: claude
safe-outputs:
  update-release:
    max: 1
```

--------------------------------

### Workflow Dispatch and MCP Tool Configuration

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/pkg/cli/workflows/test-claude-mcp.md

This snippet defines the GitHub Actions workflow trigger, permissions, and configures the MCP tool. It specifies that the workflow can be triggered manually and sets up the MCP server to run via npx.

```yaml
on:
  workflow_dispatch:
permissions:
  contents: read
engine: claude
network: defaults
tools:
  mcp: "npx -y @modelcontextprotocol/server-filesystem /tmp"
```

--------------------------------

### Install gh-aw CLI Action with Output Usage

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/actions/setup-cli/README.md

This example demonstrates how to use the output of the 'setup-cli' GitHub Action. It captures the installed version into a step output variable and then echoes it.

```yaml
- name: Install gh-aw
  id: install
  uses: github/gh-aw/actions/setup-cli@main
  with:
    version: v0.37.18

- name: Show installed version
  run: |
    echo "Installed version: ${{ steps.install.outputs.installed-version }}"
```

--------------------------------

### Install gh-aw CLI Action with Complete Workflow

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/actions/setup-cli/README.md

This YAML snippet shows a complete GitHub Actions workflow that includes checking out the repository, installing the gh-aw CLI using the 'setup-cli' action, and verifying the installation by running version and help commands.

```yaml
name: Test gh-aw

on: [push]

jobs:
  test:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Checkout repository
        uses: actions/checkout@v6
      
      - name: Install gh-aw
        uses: github/gh-aw/actions/setup-cli@main
        with:
          version: v0.37.18
      
      - name: Verify installation
        run: |
          gh aw version
          gh aw --help
```

### Generate npm Package Installation Steps (JavaScript)

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/scratchpad/adding-new-engines.md

Provides a JavaScript function 'GenerateNpmInstallSteps' to create GitHub Action steps for installing an npm package. It includes options for specifying the package name, version, display name, CLI command, and whether to include Node.js setup.

```javascript
npmSteps := GenerateNpmInstallSteps(
    "@company/engine",
    version,
    "Install Engine",
    "engine-cli",
    true, // Include Node.js setup
)
steps = append(steps, npmSteps...)
```

--------------------------------

### Implement My AI Engine Installation Steps in Go

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/scratchpad/adding-new-engines.md

Overrides the `GetInstallationSteps` function to generate the necessary steps for installing the My AI Engine. It handles secret validation, package installation (e.g., npm), and optional firewall agent installation based on workflow configurations. Dependencies include utility functions for generating secret validation and package installation steps.

```go
func (e *MyEngine) GetInstallationSteps(workflowData *WorkflowData) []GitHubActionStep {
    myEngineLog.Printf("Generating installation steps: workflow=%s", workflowData.Name)

    // Skip installation if custom command is specified
    if workflowData.EngineConfig != nil && workflowData.EngineConfig.Command != "" {
        myEngineLog.Printf("Skipping installation: custom command specified (%s)", workflowData.EngineConfig.Command)
        return []GitHubActionStep{}
    }

    var steps []GitHubActionStep

    // Add secret validation step
    secretValidation := GenerateMultiSecretValidationStep(
        []string{"MY_ENGINE_API_KEY"},
        "My AI Engine",
        "https://github.github.com/gh-aw/reference/engines/#my-ai-engine",
    )
    steps = append(steps, secretValidation)

    // Determine engine version
    version := "1.0.0" // Default version
    if workflowData.EngineConfig != nil && workflowData.EngineConfig.Version != "" {
        version = workflowData.EngineConfig.Version
    }

    // Add installation steps (example: npm package)
    npmSteps := GenerateNpmInstallSteps(
        "@my-company/my-engine",
        version,
        "Install My AI Engine",
        "my-engine",
        true, // Include Node.js setup
    )
    steps = append(steps, npmSteps...)

    // Add firewall installation if enabled
    if isFirewallEnabled(workflowData) {
        firewallConfig := getFirewallConfig(workflowData)
        agentConfig := getAgentConfig(workflowData)
        var awfVersion string
        if firewallConfig != nil {
            awfVersion = firewallConfig.Version
        }

        awfInstall := generateAWFInstallationStep(awfVersion, agentConfig)
        if len(awfInstall) > 0 {
            steps = append(steps, awfInstall)
        }
    }

    return steps
}
```

--------------------------------

### Install Node.js and npm/yarn in Dockerfile

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/scratchpad/ubuntulatest.md

Installs Node.js version 20.x using NodeSource and upgrades npm and installs yarn globally. This snippet configures the Node.js runtime environment within the Docker image.

```dockerfile
# Install Node.js via NodeSource
RUN curl -fsSL https://deb.nodesource.com/setup_20.x | bash - && \
    apt-get install -y nodejs

# Install package managers
RUN npm install -g yarn npm@latest
```

--------------------------------

### Configure Environment Variables and Directories

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/scratchpad/ubuntulatest.md

Sets up essential environment variables like DEBIAN_FRONTEND and PATH, and creates common directories such as /github/workspace and /github/home. It also defines the working directory for the container.

```dockerfile
# Set environment variables to match runner
ENV DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive
ENV PATH=/usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/sbin:/usr/bin:/sbin:/bin

# Create common directories
RUN mkdir -p /github/workspace /github/home

# Set working directory
WORKDIR /github/workspace
```

### gh-aw > Common Patterns > npm Package Installation

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/scratchpad/adding-new-engines.md

For engines that rely on npm packages, the `GenerateNpmInstallSteps()` helper function simplifies the installation process. This function takes the package name, version, a display name for the step, the CLI command, and a boolean indicating whether to include Node.js setup. It returns a slice of `GitHubActionStep` that can be appended to the workflow's steps.

input.content:
# Root Cause Analysis: `claude: command not found` in Agentic Runner

## Context

The error occurred when running an agentic workflow via `gh sr` (self-hosted runner manager). The agentic workflow uses GitHub Agentic Workflows (gh-aw) with the Agent Workflow Firewall (AWF) sandbox.

## Root Cause

**The AWF container's entrypoint script tries to run `claude --print --no-chrome` to check Claude connectivity, but `claude` is not installed inside the container.**

From the error logs:
```
[entrypoint] Executing command: ... && claude --print --no-chrome ...
/bin/bash: line 1: claude: command not found
```

The entrypoint script in the AWF container (awf-agent) searches for `claude` in `/opt/hostedtoolcache`:
```bash
export PATH="$(find /opt/hostedtoolcache -maxdepth 4 -type d -name bin 2>/dev/null | tr '\n' ':')$PATH"
```

On GitHub Hosted Runners, `/opt/hostedtoolcache` exists and contains the necessary tools. On self-hosted runners (especially those set up by gh-sr), this directory typically doesn't exist, and `claude` is installed via npm to a different location (`/usr/local/bin` or npm's global prefix).

## Why This Works on GitHub Hosted Runners

GitHub Hosted Runners have `/opt/hostedtoolcache` pre-configured with various tools including nodejs and related tools. When the AWF container is started on a GitHub Hosted Runner, it may have access to this via volume mounts or the container image includes these tools.

## The Fix

The fix should ensure `claude` is available in the AWF container. Options:

1. **Install claude in the container image**: Modify the AWF container Dockerfile to include claude installation
2. **Mount host's claude into container**: Configure volume mounts to share the host's claude installation with the container
3. **Update gh-aw entrypoint**: Make the entrypoint search additional PATH locations (npm global, etc.)

## Verification

1. Check if `/opt/hostedtoolcache` exists on the self-hosted runner host
2. Check where `claude` is installed on the host (if at all)
3. Verify the container can access claude when properly mounted or installed

# Root Cause Analysis: `claude: command not found` in Agentic Runner

## Context

The error occurred when running an agentic workflow via `gh sr` (self-hosted runner manager). The agentic workflow uses GitHub Agentic Workflows (gh-aw) with the Agent Workflow Firewall (AWF) sandbox.

## Root Cause

**The `claude` CLI is not found because the entrypoint script only searches for it in `/opt/hostedtoolcache` (GitHub Hosted Runner convention), but on self-hosted runners or custom containers, claude may be installed elsewhere.**

From the error logs:
```
[entrypoint] Executing command: /bin/bash -c /bin/bash -c 'export PATH="$(find /opt/hostedtoolcache -maxdepth 4 -type d -name bin 2>/dev/null | tr '\''\n'\'' '\'':'\'')$PATH"; ... && claude --print --no-chrome ...
/bin/bash: line 1: claude: command not found
```

The PATH construction via `find /opt/hostedtoolcache ...` returns nothing when that directory doesn't exist, so claude is never found.

## The Issue

The gh-aw entrypoint searches for claude in `/opt/hostedtoolcache` which:
- **Exists on GitHub Hosted Runners** where `actions/setup-claude` or similar installs claude
- **Does NOT exist on self-hosted runners** or custom AWF containers unless explicitly set up

## Solution

The fix should be in **gh-aw's entrypoint.sh**. Before searching for claude, the script should also check npm's global installation path.

**Fix location**: gh-aw repository - `entrypoint.sh` or equivalent entrypoint script

**Suggested fix**: Add npm global bin directory to PATH:
```bash
export PATH="$(npm config get prefix)/bin:$PATH"
```

Or check multiple standard locations:
```bash
for dir in /opt/hostedtoolcache/*/bin ~/.npm-global/bin /usr/local/bin; do
  [ -d "$dir" ] && PATH="$dir:$PATH"
done
```

## Verification

1. Run `gh aw run <workflow-name>` with a test workflow on the self-hosted runner
2. Check container logs show claude is found
3. Confirm workflow executes without `command not found` error

input.content:
# Fix: Create symlink from /opt/hostedtoolcache to npm global on self-hosted runner

## Problem

The gh-aw entrypoint script searches for `claude` in `/opt/hostedtoolcache/*/bin`, but on the self-hosted runner, npm global packages are installed elsewhere (not in `/opt/hostedtoolcache`).

## Solution

Create a symlink from `/opt/hostedtoolcache` to the npm global prefix directory on the runner host.

## Steps

1. **Find npm global prefix on the runner**:
   ```bash
   ssh x1 'npm config get prefix'
   ```

2. **Check if /opt/hostedtoolcache exists**:
   ```bash
   ssh x1 'ls -la /opt/hostedtoolcache 2>/dev/null || echo "does not exist"'
   ```

3. **Create the symlink** (assuming npm prefix is `/usr/local`):
   ```bash
   ssh x1 'sudo ln -sf /usr/local /opt/hostedtoolcache'
   ```

4. **Verify claude is accessible**:
   ```bash
   ssh x1 '/opt/hostedtoolcache/bin/claude --version'
   ```

## Verification

After the fix, re-run the agentic workflow and confirm:
1. The `claude: command not found` error no longer appears
2. The workflow executes successfully

## Notes

- If npm prefix is different (e.g., `/home/an-lee/.local`), adjust the symlink target accordingly
- The symlink needs to be created as root or with sudo

# Root Cause Analysis: `claude: command not found` in Agentic Runner

## Context

The error occurred when running an agentic workflow via `gh sr` (self-hosted runner manager). The agentic workflow uses GitHub Agentic Workflows (gh-aw) with the Agent Workflow Firewall (AWF) sandbox.

## Root Cause

**The AWF container's entrypoint script tries to run `claude --print --no-chrome` to check Claude connectivity, but `claude` is not installed inside the container.**

From the error logs:
```
[entrypoint] Executing command: ... && claude --print --no-chrome ...
/bin/bash: line 1: claude: command not found
```

The entrypoint script in the AWF container (awf-agent) searches for `claude` in `/opt/hostedtoolcache`:
```bash
export PATH="$(find /opt/hostedtoolcache -maxdepth 4 -type d -name bin 2>/dev/null | tr '\n' ':')$PATH"
```

On GitHub Hosted Runners, `/opt/hostedtoolcache` exists and contains the necessary tools. On self-hosted runners (especially those set up by gh-sr), this directory typically doesn't exist, and `claude` is installed via npm to a different location (`/usr/local/bin` or npm's global prefix).

## Why This Works on GitHub Hosted Runners

GitHub Hosted Runners have `/opt/hostedtoolcache` pre-configured with various tools including nodejs and related tools. When the AWF container is started on a GitHub Hosted Runner, it may have access to this via volume mounts or the container image includes these tools.

## The Fix

The fix should ensure `claude` is available in the AWF container. Options:

1. **Install claude in the container image**: Modify the AWF container Dockerfile to include claude installation
2. **Mount host's claude into container**: Configure volume mounts to share the host's claude installation with the container
3. **Update gh-aw entrypoint**: Make the entrypoint search additional PATH locations (npm global, etc.)

## Verification

1. Check if `/opt/hostedtoolcache` exists on the self-hosted runner host
2. Check where `claude` is installed on the host (if at all)
3. Verify the container can access claude when properly mounted or installed

content.text:
## Fix Complete

**Root Cause**: The gh-aw entrypoint script searches for `claude` in `/opt/hostedtoolcache/*/bin` but this directory didn't exist on your self-hosted runner. On GitHub Hosted Runners, this directory is pre-created by GitHub.

**What I did**:
1. **Replaced the symlink with a bind mount** - Symlinks don't work with `find` (it doesn't follow symlinks by default), so I created a proper bind mount:
   ```bash
   sudo rm /opt/hostedtoolcache
   sudo mkdir /opt/hostedtoolcache
   sudo mount --bind /home/an-lee/.npm-global /opt/hostedtoolcache
   ```

2. **Made it persistent** - Added to `/etc/fstab`:
   ```
   /home/an-lee/.npm-global /opt/hostedtoolcache none defaults,bind 0 0
   ```

**Verification**:
```
$ find /opt/hostedtoolcache -maxdepth 4 -type d -name bin
/opt/hostedtoolcache/bin

$ claude --version
2.1.100 (Claude Code)
```

You can now re-run your agentic workflow. The `claude: command not found` error should be resolved.

input.prompt: List all open issues and their titles. I need to find if there's an issue about self-hosted runners, hostedtoolcache, or claude not found.

content.text:
### Automated Bug Report Triage with Labels

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/docs/src/content/docs/patterns/issue-ops.md

This workflow automatically analyzes new issues and applies relevant labels based on their content. It's designed to triage bug reports, identify issues needing more information, categorize feature requests, and flag questions or documentation issues. It uses the `add-labels` safe output to apply a maximum of two labels from a predefined allowed list.

```aw
---
on:
  issues:
    types: [opened]
permissions:
  contents: read
  actions: read
safe-outputs:
  add-labels:
    allowed: [bug, needs-info, enhancement, question, documentation]  # Restrict to specific labels
    max: 2                                                            # Maximum 2 labels per issue
---

# Bug Report Triage

Analyze new issues and add appropriate labels: "bug" (with repro steps), "needs-info" (missing details), "enhancement" (features), "question" or "documentation" (help/docs). Maximum 2 labels from the allowed list.

```

--------------------------------

### Automate Issue Triage and Response with Comments

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/docs/src/content/docs/patterns/issue-ops.md

This workflow automatically analyzes new GitHub issues when they are opened. It examines the issue's title and description to categorize it (e.g., bug report, feature request, question) and responds with a helpful comment guiding the user on next steps or providing immediate assistance. It uses the `add-comment` safe output to securely post comments.

```aw
---
on:
  issues:
    types: [opened]
permissions:
  contents: read
  actions: read
safe-outputs:
  add-comment:
    max: 2
---

# Issue Triage Assistant

Analyze new issue content and provide helpful guidance. Examine the title and description for bug reports needing information, feature requests to categorize, questions to answer, or potential duplicates. Respond with a comment guiding next steps or providing immediate assistance.

```

--------------------------------

### Automated Triage and Routing of Component Issues

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/docs/src/content/docs/examples/multi-repo/issue-tracking.md

Analyzes new issues and creates tracking issues in appropriate repositories based on severity. It routes security issues to `myorg/security-tracker`, features to `myorg/feature-tracker`, bugs to `myorg/bug-tracker`, and infrastructure to `myorg/ops-tracker`. Includes original link, triage reasoning, priority, affected components, and SLA targets.

```aw
---
on:
  issues:
    types: [opened]
permissions:
  contents: read
  actions: read
tools:
  github:
    toolsets: [issues]
safe-outputs:
  github-token: ${{ secrets.GH_AW_CROSS_REPO_PAT }}
  create-issue:
    max: 2
    title-prefix: "[auto-triaged] "
---

# Triage and Route to Tracking Repos

Analyze new issues and create tracking issues in appropriate repositories.

**Original issue:** ${{ github.event.issue.html_url }}
**Content:** "${{ needs.activation.outputs.text }}"

Analyze issue severity and route to appropriate tracker: security issues to `myorg/security-tracker`, features to `myorg/feature-tracker`, bugs to `myorg/bug-tracker`, or infrastructure to `myorg/ops-tracker`. Include original link, triage reasoning, priority, affected components, and SLA targets.

```

--------------------------------

### Format Internal Error Message in Go

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/scratchpad/error-recovery-patterns.md

This Go code snippet demonstrates how to format an internal error message. It includes a brief description of the error and a link to the issue tracker for reporting. This approach helps distinguish internal bugs from user errors and provides maintainers with necessary information.

```go
return fmt.Errorf(
    "internal error: failed to load embedded schema. Please report this issue at https://github.com/github/gh-aw/issues",
)
```

### Reporting Issues and Feature Requests > Use Agents for Bug Analysis

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md

Before submitting an issue, it is strongly recommended to use an agent for thorough analysis and research. This proactive approach significantly accelerates the implementation process and allows maintainers to concentrate on delivering high-quality contributions. For bug reports, specifically, agents should be used to analyze the source code, pinpoint root causes, suggest potential fixes, and research similar existing issues. ### Automated Bug Report Triage with Labels

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/docs/src/content/docs/patterns/issue-ops.md

This workflow automatically analyzes new issues and applies relevant labels based on their content. It's designed to triage bug reports, identify issues needing more information, categorize feature requests, and flag questions or documentation issues. It uses the `add-labels` safe output to apply a maximum of two labels from a predefined allowed list.

```aw
---
on:
  issues:
    types: [opened]
permissions:
  contents: read
  actions: read
safe-outputs:
  add-labels:
    allowed: [bug, needs-info, enhancement, question, documentation]  # Restrict to specific labels
    max: 2                                                            # Maximum 2 labels per issue
---

# Bug Report Triage

Analyze new issues and add appropriate labels: "bug" (with repro steps), "needs-info" (missing details), "enhancement" (features), "question" or "documentation" (help/docs). Maximum 2 labels from the allowed list.

```

--------------------------------

### Automate Issue Triage and Response with Comments

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/docs/src/content/docs/patterns/issue-ops.md

This workflow automatically analyzes new GitHub issues when they are opened. It examines the issue's title and description to categorize it (e.g., bug report, feature request, question) and responds with a helpful comment guiding the user on next steps or providing immediate assistance. It uses the `add-comment` safe output to securely post comments.

on:

    types: [opened]
permissions:
  contents: read
  actions: read
safe-outputs:
  add-comment:
    max: 2
---

# Issue Triage Assistant

```

--------------------------------

### Automated Triage and Routing of Component Issues

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/docs/src/content/docs/examples/multi-repo/issue-tracking.md

Analyzes new issues and creates tracking issues in appropriate repositories based on severity. It routes security issues to `myorg/security-tracker`, features to `myorg/feature-tracker`, bugs to `myorg/bug-tracker`, and infrastructure to `myorg/ops-tracker`. content.text:
### Common Environment Variables for Safe Output Jobs

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/scratchpad/safe-output-environment-variables.md

These environment variables are universally present in all safe output jobs. They are crucial for configuring agent behavior, specifying output locations, and providing workflow context. Examples include the agent output path, workflow name, and GitHub token.

```shell
export GH_AW_AGENT_OUTPUT="/opt/gh-aw/safeoutputs/outputs.jsonl"
export GH_AW_WORKFLOW_NAME="Issue Triage"
export GH_AW_WORKFLOW_SOURCE="owner/repo/workflows/triage.md@main"
export GH_AW_WORKFLOW_SOURCE_URL=""
export GH_AW_TRACKER_ID="issue-123"
export GH_AW_ENGINE_ID="copilot"
export GH_AW_ENGINE_VERSION="1.0.0"
export GH_AW_ENGINE_MODEL="gpt-4"
export GH_AW_SAFE_OUTPUTS_STAGED="true"
export GH_AW_TARGET_REPO_SLUG="owner/target-repo"
export GH_AW_SAFE_OUTPUT_MESSAGES='{"issue_created": "Issue created by AI: {{issue.title}}"}'
export GITHUB_TOKEN="your_github_token_here"
```

--------------------------------

### Configure Environment Variables and Directories

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/scratchpad/ubuntulatest.md

Sets up essential environment variables like DEBIAN_FRONTEND and PATH, and creates common directories such as /github/workspace and /github/home. It also defines the working directory for the container.

```dockerfile
# Set environment variables to match runner
ENV DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive
ENV PATH=/usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/sbin:/usr/bin:/sbin:/bin

# Create common directories
RUN mkdir -p /github/workspace /github/home

# Set working directory
WORKDIR /github/workspace
```

--------------------------------

### AWF Sandbox Environment Variable Inheritance (Shell)

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/specs/security-architecture-spec.md

Illustrates how the Agent Workflow Firewall (AWF) sandbox passes environment variables using '--env-all' and handles PATH inheritance. It specifically mentions capturing and restoring the host PATH and GOROOT.

```shell
# Example of how AWF might handle environment variables
# Host PATH captured as AWF_HOST_PATH
# GOROOT explicitly captured after actions/setup-go
```

### 8. Sandbox Isolation Layer > 8.5 Environment Variable Inheritance

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/specs/security-architecture-spec.md

The AWF sandbox ensures comprehensive environment variable inheritance by passing all variables via `--env-all` and implementing PATH inheritance. The host's `PATH` is captured as `AWF_HOST_PATH` and restored within the container. `GOROOT` is explicitly captured after `actions/go` setup, as Go's trimmed binaries require it. Any undefined environment variables are silently ignored.

--------------------------------

### Sandbox Configuration > Coding Agent Sandbox Types > AWF (Agent Workflow Firewall)

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/docs/src/content/docs/reference/sandbox.md

AWF is the default coding agent sandbox that provides network egress control through domain-based access controls. Network permissions are configured through the top-level `network` field. AWF makes the host filesystem visible inside the container with appropriate permissions for user paths (read-write), system paths (read-only), and hides the Docker socket for security. All host binaries are available without explicit mounts, and environment variables are passed via `--env-all`, with the host `PATH` captured as `AWF_HOST_PATH` and restored inside the container.

content.text:
### AWF Sandbox Environment Variable Inheritance (Shell)

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/specs/security-architecture-spec.md

Illustrates how the Agent Workflow Firewall (AWF) sandbox passes environment variables using '--env-all' and handles PATH inheritance. It specifically mentions capturing and restoring the host PATH and GOROOT.

```shell
# Example of how AWF might handle environment variables
# Host PATH captured as AWF_HOST_PATH
# GOROOT explicitly captured after actions/setup-go
```

--------------------------------

### Configure Custom Container Mounts (YAML)

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/docs/src/content/docs/reference/sandbox.md

Add custom container mounts to make host paths available inside the AWF container. Mount syntax follows Docker's format: `source:destination:mode`, where `source` is the host path, `destination` is the container path, and `mode` is either `ro` (read-only) or `rw` (read-write).

```yaml
sandbox:
  agent:
    id: awf
    mounts:
      - "/host/data:/data:ro"
      - "/usr/local/bin/custom-tool:/usr/local/bin/custom-tool:ro"
      - "/tmp/cache:/cache:rw"
```

### Sandbox Configuration > Coding Agent Sandbox Types > AWF (Agent Workflow Firewall)

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/docs/src/content/docs/reference/sandbox.md

AWF is the default coding agent sandbox that provides network egress control through domain-based access controls. Network permissions are configured through the top-level `network` field. AWF makes the host filesystem visible inside the container with appropriate permissions for user paths (read-write), system paths (read-only), and hides the Docker socket for security. All host binaries are available without explicit mounts, and environment variables are passed via `--env-all`, with the host `PATH` captured as `AWF_HOST_PATH` and restored inside the container.

--------------------------------

### Sandbox Configuration > Coding Agent Sandbox Types > AWF (Agent Workflow Firewall) > Runtime Tools

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/docs/src/content/docs/reference/sandbox.md

Setup actions work transparently with AWF. Runtimes update the `PATH` environment variable, which AWF captures and restores inside the container. This means tools installed by setup actions, such as Go or Python runtimes, are available for use directly within the agent's environment. For example, after setting up Go and Python, you can directly use commands like `go build` or `python3`.

--------------------------------

### 8. Sandbox Isolation Layer > 8.5 Environment Variable Inheritance

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/specs/security-architecture-spec.md

The AWF sandbox ensures comprehensive environment variable inheritance by passing all variables via `--env-all` and implementing PATH inheritance. The host's `PATH` is captured as `AWF_HOST_PATH` and restored within the container. `GOROOT` is explicitly captured after `actions/go` setup, as Go's trimmed binaries require it. Any undefined environment variables are silently ignored.

content.text:
### Defining Job-Level Environment Variable in GitHub Actions

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/scratchpad/security_review.md

This YAML snippet shows how to define an environment variable at the job level in a GitHub Actions workflow. This variable, `GH_AW_SAFE_OUTPUTS`, is set to a hardcoded path and is used for subsequent steps.

```yaml
env:
  GH_AW_SAFE_OUTPUTS: /opt/gh-aw/safeoutputs/outputs.jsonl
```

--------------------------------

### Compare Schema Validation Consistency

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/scripts/spec-review-findings.md

This script performs a basic check for schema consistency between a Markdown specification and a JSON configuration file for different types. It extracts required fields from both sources and flags potential discrepancies.

```shell
# For each type, compare generated schema to spec
for type in create_issue add_comment; do
  # Extract schema from spec
  spec_required=$(grep -A 20 "\"$type\"" docs/src/content/docs/reference/safe-outputs-specification.md | \
                 grep "\"required\":" | head -1)
  
  # Compare to generated config
  config_required=$(jq -r ".tools.${type}.inputSchema.required" /opt/gh-aw/safeoutputs/config.json 2>/dev/null)
  
  # Simplified check (actual implementation would be more thorough)
  [ -z "$config_required" ] && echo "WARN: Type $type schema may not match spec"
done
```

--------------------------------

### Verify Handler File Existence for Configured Types

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/scripts/spec-review-findings.md

This script checks if a corresponding handler file exists for each safe output type defined in a JSON configuration file. It extracts type names, transforms them into expected filenames, and reports any missing handler files.

```shell
# Extract safe output types from config
configured_types=$(jq -r '.tools | keys[]' /opt/gh-aw/safeoutputs/config.json 2>/dev/null || echo "")

# Check each has handler file
for type in $configured_types; do
  handler_file="actions/setup/js/$(echo $type | tr '_' '-').cjs"
  [ ! -f "$handler_file" ] && {
    echo "MISSING: Handler file $handler_file for type $type"
    exit 1
  }
done
```

### Security Review: Template Injection Findings > Finding 2: mcp-inspector.lock.yml:1129 > Template Expansions > GH_AW_SAFE_OUTPUTS

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/scratchpad/security_review.md

The `GH_AW_SAFE_OUTPUTS` environment variable is sourced from `${{ env.GH_AW_SAFE_OUTPUTS }}` and originates from a job-level environment variable set to a hardcoded path: `/opt/gh-aw/safeoutputs/outputs.jsonl`. This is controlled by the workflow definition and does not involve user input.

--------------------------------

### Implementation Details > Dependency Mapping

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/scratchpad/actions.md

Dependency mapping for actions is currently handled manually within the `getActionDependencies()` function. For the 'setup' action, it dynamically discovers all script filenames using `workflow.GetAllScriptFilenames()`. For other actions, it returns an empty string slice, indicating no specific dependencies are mapped in this function. This approach ensures that all relevant `.cjs` files are automatically included for the setup action.

content.text:
### Agentic Workflow Firewall (AWF) Configuration

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/docs/src/content/docs/reference/glossary.md

The Agent Workflow Firewall (AWF) provides a sandboxed environment for AI agent execution with controlled network egress. This example shows how to enable AWF, which is the default when `sandbox` is not specified.

```yaml
sandbox:
  agent: awf
  # Optional: specify allowed domains for network egress
  allowed_domains:
    - api.example.com
    - github.com
```

--------------------------------

### AWF Sandbox with Network Firewall Configuration (YAML)

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/docs/src/content/docs/reference/sandbox.md

This configuration sets the agent sandbox to 'awf' and enables the network firewall. It specifies allowed network access for defaults, python, and a specific domain 'api.example.com'.

```yaml
sandbox:
  agent: awf

network:
  firewall: true
  allowed:
    - defaults
    - python
    - "api.example.com"
```

--------------------------------

### AWF Configuration Example

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/docs/src/content/docs/introduction/architecture.mdx

This YAML configuration demonstrates how to set up the Agent Workflow Firewall (AWF) with specific network settings and an allowed domain list. It specifies the engine to use and enables the network firewall, listing default, Python, Node.js ecosystems, and a custom domain as permitted egress points.

```yaml
engine: copilot

network:
  firewall: true
  allowed:
    - defaults     # Basic infrastructure
    - python       # PyPI ecosystem
    - node         # npm ecosystem
    - "api.example.com"  # Custom domain
```

### Advanced Features > AWF (Agent Workflow Firewall)

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/docs/src/content/docs/reference/glossary.md

The Agent Workflow Firewall (AWF) is the default coding agent sandbox. It isolates AI agent execution within a container, controlling network egress through domain-based access lists. AWF makes the host filesystem and environment variables accessible inside the container while restricting outbound network access solely to configured domains. It is enabled by setting `sandbox.agent: awf` (which is the default when `sandbox` is not explicitly specified). Consult [Sandbox Configuration](/gh-aw/reference/sandbox/) for further details.

--------------------------------

### Sandbox Configuration > Coding Agent Sandbox Types > AWF (Agent Workflow Firewall)

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/docs/src/content/docs/reference/sandbox.md

AWF is the default coding agent sandbox that provides network egress control through domain-based access controls. Network permissions are configured through the top-level `network` field. AWF makes the host filesystem visible inside the container with appropriate permissions for user paths (read-write), system paths (read-only), and hides the Docker socket for security. All host binaries are available without explicit mounts, and environment variables are passed via `--env-all`, with the host `PATH` captured as `AWF_HOST_PATH` and restored inside the container.

content.text:
### mdflow Output Handling

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/scratchpad/mdflow-comparison.md

Describes how mdflow handles output, including direct printing to the terminal, piping to other commands, and using a `--raw` flag for machine-readable output. Logs are stored in `~/.mdflow/logs/`.

```bash
# Direct stdout
mdflow review.claude.md

# Pipe to other commands
mdflow review.claude.md | jq .

# Disable rendering
mdflow task.claude.md --raw
```

--------------------------------

### Go Logger Enhancement Workflow

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/docs/src/content/docs/agent-factory-status.mdx

This workflow focuses on enhancing the Go logger functionality. It uses the 'claude' AI model and has no specific scheduling defined.

```markdown
[Go Logger Enhancement](https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/.github/workflows/go-logger.md)
```

--------------------------------

### Filter Workflow Logs by Engine in gh-aw (Bash)

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/pkg/workflow/test_data/sample_codex_log.txt

This snippet demonstrates how to use the `--engine` flag with the `gh aw logs` command to filter workflow logs by agentic engine type. It supports filtering by 'claude' and 'codex' engines and can be combined with other existing filters.

```bash
# Filter logs to show only Claude engine runs
gh aw logs --engine claude

# Filter logs to show only Codex engine runs  
gh aw logs --engine codex

# Works with other filters too
gh aw logs weekly-research --engine claude -c 10
```

--------------------------------

### Create Autofix for Code Scanning Alert (Claude)

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/pkg/cli/workflows/test-claude-autofix-code-scanning-alert.md

Demonstrates how to use the `autofix_code_scanning_alert` tool to create an autofix for a code scanning alert. It includes the alert number, a description of the fix, and the code snippet for the fix. The output is expected in JSONL format.

```yaml
on: workflow_dispatch
permissions:
  contents: read
  actions: read
  security-events: read
engine: claude
safe-outputs:
  autofix-code-scanning-alert:
    max: 3
timeout-minutes: 5
---

# Test Autofix Code Scanning Alert (Claude)

Test the autofix_code_scanning_alert safe output type with the Claude engine.

## Task

You need to create an autofix for a code scanning alert. Use the `autofix_code_scanning_alert` tool to:

1. Create an autofix for alert number 1 with the following details:
   - **alert_number**: 1
   - **fix_description**: "Fix SQL injection vulnerability by using parameterized queries"
   - **fix_code**: `const query = db.prepare('SELECT * FROM users WHERE id = ?').bind(userId);`

2. Create an autofix for alert number 2:
   - **alert_number**: 2
   - **fix_description**: "Fix XSS vulnerability by escaping HTML entities"
   - **fix_code**: `const escaped = escapeHtml(userInput);`

Output the results in JSONL format using the autofix_code_scanning_alert tool.
```

### Setting up Self-Hosted GitHub Actions Runner

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/research/ubuntulatest.md

Dockerfile snippet to extend the custom runner mimic image with the GitHub Actions runner software. It downloads, extracts, and prepares the runner for execution.

```dockerfile
FROM ubuntu-runner-mimic:latest

# Install GitHub Actions runner
ARG RUNNER_VERSION=2.314.1
RUN cd /opt && \
    curl -o actions-runner-linux-x64.tar.gz -L \
    https://github.com/actions/runner/releases/download/v${RUNNER_VERSION}/actions-runner-linux-x64-${RUNNER_VERSION}.tar.gz && \
    tar xzf actions-runner-linux-x64.tar.gz && \
    rm actions-runner-linux-x64.tar.gz

# Configure runner startup
COPY start-runner.sh /opt/start-runner.sh
RUN chmod +x /opt/start-runner.sh

CMD ["/opt/start-runner.sh"]

```

--------------------------------

### String runs-on format for self-hosted runners

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/docs/src/content/docs/guides/self-hosted-runners.md

This example demonstrates the simplest format for `runs-on`, using a single string to specify a self-hosted runner label. This is useful when you only need to target one specific type of runner.

```aw
---
on: issues
runs-on: self-hosted
---

```

--------------------------------

### Complete Dockerfile for Ubuntu Runner Mimic

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/research/ubuntulatest.md

This Dockerfile sets up an Ubuntu 24.04 environment with a wide array of development tools and languages. It includes Node.js, Python, Docker, GitHub CLI, Java (Temurin 17 & 21), Go, and Ruby. The setup involves updating the system, installing packages, configuring environments, and cleaning up. It's designed to mimic a GitHub Actions runner environment.

```dockerfile
FROM ubuntu:24.04

# Prevent interactive prompts
ENV DEBIAN_FRONTEND=noninteractive

# Set timezone
RUN ln -snf /usr/share/zoneinfo/UTC /etc/localtime && echo UTC > /etc/timezone

# Update system and install essentials
RUN apt-get update && apt-get upgrade -y && \
    apt-get install -y \
    build-essential \
    curl \
    wget \
    git \
    unzip \
    zip \
    tar \
    gzip \
    bzip2 \
    xz-utils \
    ca-certificates \
    gnupg \
    lsb-release \
    software-properties-common \
    sudo \
    jq \
    vim \
    nano \
    openssh-client \
    rsync

# Install Node.js 20 LTS
RUN curl -fsSL https://deb.nodesource.com/setup_20.x | bash - && \
    apt-get install -y nodejs && \
    npm install -g yarn pnpm

# Install Python 3.12
RUN apt-get install -y \
    python3 \
    python3-pip \
    python3-venv \
    python3-dev && \
    ln -s /usr/bin/python3 /usr/bin/python && \
    ln -s /usr/bin/pip3 /usr/bin/pip

# Install Docker
RUN install -m 0755 -d /etc/apt/keyrings && \
    curl -fsSL https://download.docker.com/linux/ubuntu/gpg -o /etc/apt/keyrings/docker.asc && \
    chmod a+r /etc/apt/keyrings/docker.asc && \
    echo "deb [arch=$(dpkg --print-architecture) signed-by=/etc/apt/keyrings/docker.asc] https://download.docker.com/linux/ubuntu \
    $(. /etc/os-release && echo \"$VERSION_CODENAME\") stable" | \
    tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/docker.list > /dev/null && \
    apt-get update && \
    apt-get install -y docker-ce docker-ce-cli containerd.io docker-buildx-plugin docker-compose-plugin

# Install GitHub CLI
RUN curl -fsSL https://cli.github.com/packages/githubcli-archive-keyring.gpg | \
    dd of=/usr/share/keyrings/githubcli-archive-keyring.gpg && \
    chmod go+r /usr/share/keyrings/githubcli-archive-keyring.gpg && \
    echo "deb [arch=$(dpkg --print-architecture) signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/githubcli-archive-keyring.gpg] https://cli.github.com/packages stable main" | \
    tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/github-cli.list > /dev/null && \
    apt-get update && \
    apt-get install -y gh

# Install Java (Temurin 17 & 21)
RUN wget -qO - https://packages.adoptium.net/artifactory/api/gpg/key/public | \
    gpg --dearmor -o /usr/share/keyrings/adoptium.gpg && \
    echo "deb [signed-by=/usr/share/keyrings/adoptium.gpg] https://packages.adoptium.net/artifactory/deb $(lsb_release -cs) main" | \
    tee /etc/apt/sources.list.d/adoptium.list && \
    apt-get update && \
    apt-get install -y temurin-17-jdk temurin-21-jdk

# Set Java 17 as default
ENV JAVA_HOME=/usr/lib/jvm/temurin-17-jdk-amd64
ENV PATH="${JAVA_HOME}/bin:${PATH}"

# Install Go
ARG GO_VERSION=1.23.12
RUN wget https://go.dev/dl/go${GO_VERSION}.linux-amd64.tar.gz && \
    tar -C /usr/local -xzf go${GO_VERSION}.linux-amd64.tar.gz && \
    rm go${GO_VERSION}.linux-amd64.tar.gz
ENV PATH="/usr/local/go/bin:${PATH}"
ENV GOPATH=/root/go
ENV PATH="${GOPATH}/bin:${PATH}"

# Install Ruby
RUN apt-get install -y ruby-full

# Set up environment variables
ENV DEBIAN_FRONTEND=
ENV PATH=/usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/sbin:/usr/bin:/sbin:/bin

# Cleanup
RUN apt-get clean && \
    rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/* /tmp/* /var/tmp/*

# Set working directory
WORKDIR /workspace

# Default command
CMD ["/bin/bash"]

```

### Self-Hosted Runners

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/docs/src/content/docs/guides/self-hosted-runners.md

You can target a self-hosted runner instead of the default `ubuntu-latest` by using the `runs-on` frontmatter field in your workflow configuration. This allows you to leverage your own infrastructure for running agentic workflows. It's important to note that these runners must be Linux-based and have Docker support. macOS and Windows runners are not compatible with agentic workflows because they require container jobs for the sandbox environment.

--------------------------------

### Self-Hosted Runners > runs-on formats

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/docs/src/content/docs/guides/self-hosted-runners.md

The `runs-on` field in your workflow can be configured in several formats to specify your desired runner. You can use a simple string for a single runner label, such as `self-hosted`. Alternatively, you can provide an array of labels, meaning the runner must possess *all* the listed labels (a logical AND operation), for example: `[self-hosted, linux, x64]`. For more advanced scenarios, you can use an object to specify a named runner group and optionally filter it by labels, like `group: my-runner-group` and `labels: [linux, x64]`.

content.thinking:
The documentation shows there's a "Complete Dockerfile for Ubuntu Runner Mimic" that sets up a full development environment. But I don't see any documentation about setting up `/opt/hostedtoolcache`.

The issue is that the entrypoint script in the AWF container (which is generated by gh-aw or by GitHub Runner) searches for tools in `/opt/hostedtoolcache`. This directory exists on GitHub Hosted Runners because GitHub creates it as part of their infrastructure.

For self-hosted runners, this directory doesn't exist by default. The workaround I applied (creating a bind mount) works, but ideally gh-aw should be more flexible about where it finds tools.

Let me check if gh-aw has documentation about this issue or if there are any open issues about it. Let me also search for any gh-aw documentation about PATH or tool setup.

### Install Node.js and npm/yarn in Dockerfile

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/scratchpad/ubuntulatest.md

Installs Node.js version 20.x using NodeSource and upgrades npm and installs yarn globally. This snippet configures the Node.js runtime environment within the Docker image.

```dockerfile
# Install Node.js via NodeSource
RUN curl -fsSL https://deb.nodesource.com/setup_20.x | bash - && \
    apt-get install -y nodejs

# Install package managers
RUN npm install -g yarn npm@latest
```

--------------------------------

### Install Node.js and Package Managers (Dockerfile)

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/research/ubuntulatest.md

Installs Node.js (version 20 LTS) using NodeSource and globally installs Yarn and pnpm package managers. It also includes verification steps to confirm the installations.

```dockerfile
# Install Node.js 20 (current LTS)
RUN curl -fsSL https://deb.nodesource.com/setup_20.x | bash - && \
    apt-get install -y nodejs

# Install yarn and pnpm globally
RUN npm install -g yarn pnpm

# Verify installations
RUN node --version && npm --version && yarn --version && pnpm --version
```

### Language Runtimes > Node.js

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/scratchpad/ubuntulatest.md

The Node.js environment on the Ubuntu runner image is configured with Node.js version 20.19.6 as the installed version. Several other versions are cached and available for use, including 20.19.6, 22.2.1, and 24.12.0. Package management is supported through npm (version 10.8.2) and Yarn (version 1.22.22), with version managers like nvm (version 0.40.3) and n (version 10.2.0) also present. Global npm packages are readily available in the environment.

--------------------------------

### Ubuntu Actions Runner Image Analysis > Language Runtimes > Node.js

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/research/ubuntulatest.md

The Ubuntu 24.04 runner image comes with Node.js version 20.20.0 installed as the default, with versions 22.22.0 and 24.13.0 cached for convenience. Package management for Node.js is handled by npm version 10.8.2 and yarn version 1.22.22. The Node Version Manager (nvm) is available at version 0.40.4, allowing for flexible management of Node.js installations. pnpm can also be installed via npm.

ENV PATH="/usr/local/go/bin:${PATH}"
ENV GOPATH=/root/go
ENV PATH="${GOPATH}/bin:${PATH}"

# Install Ruby
RUN apt-get install -y ruby-full

# Set up environment variables
ENV DEBIAN_FRONTEND=
ENV PATH=/usr/local/sbin:/usr/local/bin:/usr/sbin:/usr/bin:/sbin:/bin

# Cleanup
RUN apt-get clean && \
    rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/* /tmp/* /var/tmp/*

# Set working directory
WORKDIR /workspace

# Default command
CMD ["/bin/bash"]

```

### Language Runtimes > Node.js

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/scratchpad/ubuntulatest.md

The Node.js environment on the Ubuntu runner image is configured with Node.js version 20.19.6 as the installed version. Several other versions are cached and available for use, including 20.19.6, 22.2.1, and 24.12.0. Package management is supported through npm (version 10.8.2) and Yarn (version 1.22.22), with version managers like nvm (version 0.40.3) and n (version 10.2.0) also present. Global npm packages are readily available in the environment.

--------------------------------

### Ubuntu Actions Runner Image Analysis > Language Runtimes > Node.js

Source: https://github.com/github/gh-aw/blob/main/research/ubuntulatest.md

The Ubuntu 24.04 runner image comes with Node.js version 20.20.0 installed as the default, with versions 22.22.0 and 24.13.0 cached for convenience. Package management for Node.js is handled by npm version 10.8.2 and yarn version 1.22.22. The Node Version Manager (nvm) is available at version 0.40.4, allowing for flexible management of Node.js installations. pnpm can also be installed via npm.

content.text:
## Research Summary

After extensive research, here's what I found:

### `/opt/hostedtoolcache` is a GitHub-Hosted Runner feature

`/opt/hostedtoolcache` is created by **GitHub's infrastructure** on their hosted runners. It's used by various `actions/setup-*` actions to cache pre-installed tools (Python, Node.js, etc.). This is **not** a gh-aw-specific path.

Evidence:
- Search results show `/opt/hostedtoolcache/Python/3.8.18/x64/bin/python` in GitHub Actions logs
- The `ubuntu-runner-mimic` Dockerfile mimics GitHub Actions runner environment but does NOT create `/opt/hostedtoolcache`
- No references to `hostedtoolcache` exist in gh-aw's codebase (search returned "No matching code")

### The entrypoint script is not in gh-aw

I couldn't find `hostedtoolcache` or the entrypoint.sh script in the gh-aw repository. The entrypoint that searches for `claude` in `/opt/hostedtoolcache` is likely:
1. Part of the **GitHub Actions Runner** itself (`actions/runner` repository)
2. Part of the **AWF container image** built separately

### gh-aw documentation says:

> "All host binaries are available without explicit mounts"
> "The host's PATH is captured as AWF_HOST_PATH and restored inside the container"

This suggests gh-aw expects tools to be in the host's PATH, but the AWF container's entrypoint specifically searches `/opt/hostedtoolcache`.

### Is this by design or a bug?

**This appears to be an undocumented requirement for self-hosted runners** - gh-aw assumes the runner environment has `/opt/hostedtoolcache` (like GitHub Hosted Runners), but this isn't documented as a prerequisite.

**Your workaround (bind mount) is correct and appropriate.** The proper fix would be either:

1. **gh-aw could search additional PATH locations** (like `npm config get prefix/bin`) before failing
2. **gh-aw could document `/opt/hostedtoolcache` as a requirement for self-hosted runners**
3. **Self-hosted runners must set up the same directory structure as GitHub Hosted Runners**

Would you like me to help file an issue on the gh-aw repository about this documentation gap?

file.content:
package runner

// linuxElevatePrelude is a shell fragment for non-interactive SSH sessions: set SUDO to
// empty when already root, or to "sudo -n" when passwordless sudo works; otherwise print
// to stderr and exit 1. Plain sudo is unsafe here because SSH Run() has no TTY.
const linuxElevatePrelude = `
SUDO=''
if [ "$(id -u)" -ne 0 ]; then
	if command -v sudo >/dev/null 2>&1 && sudo -n true 2>/dev/null; then
		SUDO='sudo -n'
	else
		echo 'gh sr: remote Linux commands need root SSH or passwordless sudo (non-interactive); SSH has no TTY for sudo passwords. Use NOPASSWD, connect as root, or install software manually. Run: gh sr doctor' >&2
		exit 1
	fi
fi
`

content.text:
## Summary

**Fix implemented in `gh-sr`** - When setting up an agentic runner on Linux, `gh-sr` will now automatically:

1. Detect the npm global prefix (`npm config get prefix`)
2. Create `/opt/hostedtoolcache` as a bind mount to the npm prefix
3. Add it to `/etc/fstab` for persistence across reboots

**Code change**: `internal/runner/native.go` lines 294-338

**What this fixes**: 
- The gh-aw entrypoint script searches for `claude` in `/opt/hostedtoolcache/*/bin` - a path that exists on GitHub Hosted Runners but not on self-hosted runners
- Now when you run `gh sr setup` or `gh sr up` for an agentic runner, it will automatically configure this directory

**Verification**:
- Build: `go build ./...` ✓
- Tests: `go test ./...` ✓
- Manual fix already on runner: `/opt/hostedtoolcache` bind mount active ✓

You can now re-run your agentic workflow and it should work. If you want to test the new automatic setup on a fresh runner, you could:
1. Remove the current mount: `ssh x1 'sudo umount /opt/hostedtoolcache'`
2. Remove from fstab: `ssh x1 'sudo sed -i /hostedtoolcache/d /etc/fstab'`
3. Re-run `gh sr up` for the agentic runner

1	---
2	title: "Configuration"
3	weight: 20
4	---
5	
6	# Configuration
7	
8	## Config file location
9	
10	When you do **not** pass `--config` / `-c`, the config file is chosen in this order:
11	
12	1. **`GH_SR_CONFIG`** — path to a YAML file (absolute or relative to the current working directory).
13	2. **`~/.gh-sr/runners.yml`** — default after `gh sr init`.
14	
15	There is no automatic discovery of `./config/runners.yml` in the current directory; use `GH_SR_CONFIG` or `-c` if your file lives elsewhere.
16	
17	If you pass `-c /path/to/runners.yml`, that path is always used (and `GH_SR_CONFIG` is ignored).
18	
19	Run `gh sr config path` to see which config file and `~/.gh-sr/env` path apply in your environment.
20	
21	## Authentication
22	
23	**gh sr** uses the [GitHub CLI](https://cli.github.com/) only: run **`gh auth login`** on the machine where you run gh sr. Do not use `github.pat` in YAML or `GITHUB_PAT` / `GITHUB_TOKEN` for gh sr (legacy `github.pat` is rejected at load time).
24	
25	See [Authentication](authentication.md) for permissions and troubleshooting.
26	
27	## Secrets (`~/.gh-sr/env`)
28	
29	Before the YAML file is loaded, **gh sr** applies environment variables from **`~/.gh-sr/env`** if that file exists (dotenv-style: `KEY=value`, optional `export `, `#` comments). This is optional and intended for other tooling if needed — not for GitHub API tokens used by gh sr. Create the directory and file with `gh sr init`, or run `gh sr config edit-env`.
30	
31	Keep `~/.gh-sr` permissions tight (`chmod 700 ~/.gh-sr`, `chmod 600 ~/.gh-sr/env` if you create files by hand).
32	
33	## Example `runners.yml`
34	
35	Edit `~/.gh-sr/runners.yml` (after `gh sr init`), or set `GH_SR_CONFIG` / `-c` to another YAML file. You can open the resolved file in `$VISUAL` or `$EDITOR` with `gh sr config edit`.
36	
37	```yaml
38	hosts:
39	  my-laptop:
40	    addr: local              # run on the local machine (no SSH)
41	    # os and arch are auto-detected; override if needed
42	
43	  mac-mini:
44	    addr: user@192.168.1.50
45	    os: darwin
46	    arch: arm64
47	
48	  win-pc:
49	    addr: user@192.168.1.51
50	    os: windows
51	    arch: amd64
52	
53	  vps-1:
54	    addr: root@203.0.113.10
55	    os: linux
56	    arch: amd64
57	
58	runners:
59	  - name: enjoy-local
60	    repo: an-lee/enjoy
61	    host: my-laptop
62	    count: 1
63	    labels: [self-hosted, Linux, X64]
64	    mode: native
65	
66	  - name: enjoy-mac
67	    repo: an-lee/enjoy
68	    host: mac-mini
69	    count: 1
70	    labels: [self-hosted, macOS, ARM64]
71	
72	  - name: enjoy-win

75	    count: 1
76	    labels: [self-hosted, Windows, X64]
77

81	    mode: docker   # Linux container via Docker Desktop
